notes: Iteration 3 targets a real software project (Open-ClaudeCode, TypeScript CLI) instead of the content repository used in iterations 1-2. This is the first meaningful test of the skill's scaffolding generation capability.

evals.prompt: Assess the scaffolding state of /Users/josh_folder/Open-ClaudeCode and tell me what's missing for autonomous agent operation

analyst_observations: The skill's primary value-add is structural consistency: it enforces coverage of all 11 dimensions (E1-E4, O1-O4, V1-V3) and O1-O4 sub-assessment, which the baseline misses.
Pass rate delta (+13.3%) is driven by the skill's framework enforcing dimensional assessment (covers-all-dimensions, assesses-observability-dimensions) that the baseline skips.

The skill failed on detects-existing-telemetry in eval-1 because probe scripts check for generic patterns rather than project-specific telemetry infrastructure. This is a gap in the detect-observability.sh script.
Eval-2 (assess-only) shows the skill is 34% faster than baseline (252s vs 384s) because probe scripts shortcut the analysis. This is the skill's strongest use case.

Eval-3 shows an interesting trade-off: the without_skill baseline produced richer TypeScript source files (5 files, 2,207 lines) while the with_skill produced shell scripts (4 files, ~72KB). Different approaches, both valid.
Timing is nearly identical on average (501s vs 505s) but varies per eval: skill is faster on eval-2 (assess-only) but slower on eval-1 and eval-3 (generation with RE-ASSESS loop).

The without_skill run produced notably more creative and project-specific output (e.g., lint-import-direction.sh, analyze-deps.sh, CI pipeline YAML) that the skill's template-based approach didn't generate.
